OFFICE OF

CITY ATTORNEY
(415) 329-2171

FAX No.:
(415) 329-2646

CITY OF PALO ALTO

P.O. BOX 10250
PALO ALTO, CALIFORNIA 94303

750 HAMILTON AVENUE
PALO ALTO, CALIFORNIA 94301

January 17, 1990

Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, CA 95814

Attention: Legal Department

RE: Request for Advice Pursuant to Government Code
Section 83114(b)

Dear Ladies and Gentlemen:

I have been requested by two members of the Palo Alto
Planning Commission to submit to you for advice the question of
whether their respective interests in real property located within
300 feet of property that is the subject of a planning Commission
decision (or series of decisions) constitute conflicts of interest
so as to disqualify them from participating in such decisions.

Background:

The Palo Alto Medical Foundation ("PAMF") is proposing
to develop certain property it owns in the City of Palo Alto
("City") to expand and consolidate its facilities. Approval of the
proposed development (the "project") will require adoption of a
specific plan, amendment to the City's general plan and certain
rezoning actions. In the City of Palo Alto, the Planning Commission (the "Commission") reviews all such proposals and makes its
recommendation to the City Council.

The Commission is currently considering the proposed
specific plan and the Draft Environmental Impact Report ("DEIR")
for the proposed project. The Commission has closed the public
hearing on the DEIR, and the first of two required public hearings
on the specific plan. The Commission will be holding one or more
study sessions to discuss the specific plan and possibly to make
changes to it prior to holding the second public hearing. I would
estimate that the second public hearing will be held in approximately one month, after which the Commission will send its proposed
specific plan to the City Council. For purposes of the Political
Reform Act, the Commission's action on the specific plan will
constitute a decision.

Fair Political Practices Commission
January 17, 1990
Page 2

RE:  Government Code Section 83114(b)

The Physical Setting:

     The proposed specific plan covers a site to be developed
in three phases, within a "campus" area bounded by Ramona Street,
Channing Avenue, Waverly Street and Homer Avenue, plus a portion
of land across the street on Homer Avenue between Bryant Street and
Waverly Street.  For your information, I have enclosed three pages
from the proposed specific plan to show the proposed phased
development.  (Figures 16, 17 and 18.)  I have also enclosed Figure
6 from the proposed specific plan, which shows the lotting pattern
of the area as well as the existing land use for all lots in the
area.  On Figure 6 I have marked the lots in which the two Planning
Commissioners have an interest.  You will note that Commissioner
Cullen's townhouse development is "Catty cornered" from the project
site and that a portion of the real property in which Commissioner
Chandler has an interest backs up to that part of the proposed
project which is across the street from the proposed campus.

Legal Analysis:

     I have written a letter to each commissioner advising
that I believe each has a conflict of interest and should not
participate, for the reasons set forth in the respective letters.
I have enclosed those letters for your further information.

     The Planning Commission has asked that I request that you
use all deliberate speed in making your response, so that if either
or both of the Commissioners is found not to have a conflict, they
can participate in remaining proceedings.  If you believe that your
response will take more than thirty days, I request that you advise
me by telephone of your tentative determination, so that I can pass
on that information.  If you need any further information to make
a determination, please let me know.

     Thank you for your assistance.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        [signature]
                                        SUSAN CASE
                                        Sr. Assistant City Attorney
SWC:jm

Enclosures

CC:  Mark Chandler
     Patricia Cullen
     Ken Schreiber
     Tony Bennetti

OFFICE OF

CITY ATTORNEY                                                 FAX No.:
                (415) 329-2171        CITY OF PALO ALTO                    (415) 329-2646

P. O. BOX 10250                                              250 HAMILTON AVENUE
PALO ALTO, CALIFORNIA 94303                                 PALO ALTO, CALIFORNIA 94301


                                        January 10, 1990




Mrs. Patricia Cullen
231 Homer Street
Palo Alto, CA  94301

               RE:  Conflict of Interest

     Dear Pat:

          You and I have discussed the question of whether your
interest in certain real estate, namely, your residence, would
require that you not participate in the pending decisions affecting
the Palo Alto Medical Foundation (PAMF).  I have concluded that
your ownership interest would constitute a conflict of interest
that would require you not to so participate.

          Facts

          You and your husband own a condominium at 231 Homer
Street in Palo Alto (the "Property") which is located at the corner
of Homer and Ramona.  The Property is across the street from the
site of the proposed PAMF campus that is the subject of a proposed
Specific Plan.  For purposes of the Political Reform Act (the
"Act"; generally, Title 9 of the Government Code) your property
interest is within 300 feet of another property which is the
subject of a proposed decision by the Planning Commission.

          Legal Discussions

          The Act prohibits a public official from participating
in decisions in which he knows or has reason to know he has a
financial interest.  (Gov't Code § 87100).  "An official has a
financial interest in a decision within the meaning of Section
87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have
a material effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public
generally, on the official ... or on any source of income ...
aggregating two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value
provided to, received by or promised to the public official within
12 months prior to the time when the decision is made."  (Gov't
Code § 87103(c)).

          The Fair Political Practices Commission ("FPPCC") has
promulgated regulations to interpret whether a source of income to
a public official creates a conflict of interest in a particular

Mrs. Patricia Cullen
January 10, 1990
Page 2

RE: Conflict of Interest

situation. These regulations are set forth in Title 2 of the
California Administrative Code. In your particular situation, the
analysis is as follows:

Cal.Admn. Code § 18702.3(a)(1) states:

"The effect of a decision is material as to real property
in which an official has a direct, indirect or beneficial ownership
interest (not including a leasehold interest), if ... the real
property in which the official has an interest, or any part of that
real property, is located within a 300 foot radius of the boun-
daries (or the proposed boundaries) of the property which is the
subject of the decision, unless the decision will have no financial
effect upon the officials's real property interest."

It appears that you do, indeed, have a conflict which
would require you to disqualify yourself from participating in the
current decisions concerning the proposed development plan of the
PAMF.

As we discussed, I will submit the question of your
conflict to the FPPC for a formal ruling. You should most likely
have an answer from them prior to the next time the Commission
considers the matter. In the meantime, however, I would advise you
to not participate in further proceedings.

As this conflict has only recently come to our atten-
tion, you have previously participated in the matter. However,
your previous participation was limited to hearing a presentation
of the plan by staff and listening to public testimony. No action,
either conceptual or final, has yet been taken. Therefore, no harm
has been done.

I will advise you further when I receive the FPPC ruling.

Sincerely,

SUSAN W. CASE
Sr. Assistant City Attorney

SWC:kr

cc: Ken Schreiber
Tony Bennetti

C

OFFICE OF

CITY ATTORNEY                                                 FAX No.:
(415) 329-2171                 CITY OF PALO ALTO                    (415) 329-2646

P.O. BOX 10250                                              250 HAMILTON AVENUE
PALO ALTO, CALIFORNIA 94303                                PALO ALTO, CALIFORNIA 94301


                                                            January 10, 1990




Mr. Mark Chandler
2377 Ramona Street
Palo Alto, CA 94301

                    RE:   Conflict of Interest

     Dear Mark:

          You inquired as to whether your interest in a certain
piece of real estate would require that you not participate in the
pending decisions affecting the Palo Alto Medical Foundation
("PAMF").  I have concluded that your ownership interest would
constitute a conflict of interest that would require you not to so
participate.

     Facts

     You have stated that you have an investment in a
partnership which owns an apartment building at 360 Forest in Palo
Alto (the "Building").  The partnership owns the building and
improvements and leases the land.  The Building is adjacent to
property owned by the PAMF and lies within the study area covered
by the proposed Specific Plan.  For purposes of the Political
Reform Act (Title 9 of the Government Code) the PAMF your
interest is within 300 feet of another property which is the
subject of a proposed decision by the Planning Commission.  It
appears that the Building itself is also within the boundaries of
the Specific Plan study area, although that will not constitute a
conflict of interest unless the proposed boundaries of the actual
Specific Plan site are changed during the process.

     Legal Discussion

     The Political Reform Act (the "Act"; generally, Title 9
of the Government Code) prohibits a public official from par-
ticipating in decisions in which he knows or has reason to know he
has a financial interest. (Gov't Code § 87100).  "An official has
a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of Section
87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have
a material effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public
generally, on the official ... or on any source of income ...
aggregating two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value

Mr. Mark Chandler
January 10, 1990
Page 2

RE: Conflict of Interest

provided to, received by or promised to the public official within
12 months prior to the time when the decision is made." (Gov't
Code § 87103(c)).

The Fair Political Practices Commission ("FPPC") has
promulgated regulations to interpret whether a source of income to
a public official creates a conflict of interest in a particular
situation. These regulations are set forth in Title 2 of the
California Administrative Code. In your particular situation, the
analysis is as follows:

Cal.Admn. Code § 18702.3(a)(1) states:

"The effect of a decision is material as to real property
in which an official has a direct, indirect or beneficial ownership
interest (not including a leasehold interest), if ... the real
property in which the official has an interest, or any part of that
real property, is located within a 300 foot radius of the boun-
daries (or the proposed boundaries) of the property which is the
subject of the decision, unless the decision will have no financial
effect upon the officials's real property interest."

It appears that you do, indeed, have a conflict which
would require you to disqualify yourself from participating in the
current decisions concerning the proposed development plan of the
PAMP. However, since your interest in the real property to which
the building is affixed is somewhat unusual, I have taken the
precaution of adding the following discussion.

Definition of Real Property

For the purpose of this determination, I have assumed
that the building will come within the definition of "real
property" for purposes of the Act. The Civil Code (§ 658)
definition of "real property" includes both land and buildings
thereon. The Act does not define "real property". I acknowledge
that under certain arrangements buildings can be separated from the
land on which they are located, for specific purposes, including
taxes. I do not believe that the Act makes such a distinction;
however, at this late date, I did not have a chance to confirm this
with the Fair Political Practices Commission (FPPC). You have
indicated that your value in this building has increased in the
several years since you first invested, which seems to indicate
that its relative value is tied to the value of the land. As such,
any potential effect in the value of the land would likewise affect
the value of the building.

Mr. Mark Chandler
January 10, 1990
Page 3

RE: Conflict of Interest

If you would like me to submit this definition question
to the FPPC for a formal ruling, I will do so. You would most
likely have an answer from them prior to the next time the
Commission considers this matter. In the meantime, however, I
would advise you to not participate in further proceedings.

You mentioned that you had just remembered this ownership
interest, and wondered whether any previous participation by
created any problems, either for you or for the Commission. My
response is that your previous participation has been limited to
hearing a presentation of the plan by staff and listening to public
testimony. No action, either conceptual or final, has yet been
taken. Therefore, no harm has been done.

If you wish to discuss this matter further, or if you
wish to submit the question to the FPPC, please let me know.

Sincerely,

[signature]
SUSAN W. CASE
Sr. Assistant City Attorney

SWC:kr

CC: Ken Schreiber
Tony Bennetti

BRADY AND ASSOCIATES

sections.question_synthetic: Do two Planning Commission members have financial conflicts of interest that require them to recuse themselves from decisions on a PAMF development project, based on their ownership interests in properties within 300 feet of the project site?
sections.parsing_notes: Mixed formats: modern, ocr; LLM: Document consists of three letters: (1) primary request letter from City Attorney to FPPC dated January 17, 1990; (2) letter to Patricia Cullen dated January 10, 1990 advising of conflict; (3) letter to Mark Chandler dated January 10, 1990 advising of conflict. The City Attorney has already made preliminary determinations of conflict but is requesting formal FPPC advice. Document ID 90059 indicates this is the incoming request that would have generated an FPPC advice letter response.

embedding.qa_text: QUESTION: Whether two members of the Palo Alto Planning Commission have conflicts of interest that would disqualify them from participating in Planning Commission decisions regarding a proposed Palo Alto Medical Foundation (PAMF) development project, based on their respective interests in real property located within 300 feet of the subject property.

BOX 10250 PALO ALTO, CALIFORNIA 94303 750 HAMILTON AVENUE PALO ALTO, CALIFORNIA 94301 January 17, 1990 Fair Political Practices Commission 428 J Street, Suite 800 Sacramento, CA 95814 Attention: Legal Department RE: Request for Advice Pursuant to Government Code Section 83114(b) Dear Ladies and Gentlemen: I have been requested by two members of the Palo Alto Planning Commission to submit to you for advice the question of whether their respective interests in real property located within 300 feet of property that is the subject of a planning Commission decision (or series of decisions) constitute conflicts of interest so as to disqualify them from participating in such decisions. Background: The Palo Alto Medical Foundation ("PAMF") is proposing to develop certain property it owns in the City of Palo Alto ("City") to expand and consolidate its facilities. Approval of the proposed development (the "project") will require adoption of a specific plan, amendment to the City's general plan and certain rezoning actions. In the City of Palo Alto, the Planning Commission (the "Commission") reviews all such proposals and makes its recommendation to the City Council. The Commission is currently considering the proposed specific plan and the Draft Environmental Impact Report ("DEIR") for the proposed project. The Commission has closed the public hearing on the DEIR, and the first of two required public hearings on the specific plan. The Commission will be holding one or more study sessions to discuss the specific plan and possibly to make changes to it prior to holding the second public hearing. I would estimate that the second public hearing will be held in approximately one month, after which the Commission will send its proposed specific plan to the City Council. For purposes of the Political Reform Act, the Commission's action on the specific plan will constitute a decision. Fair Political Practices Commission January 17, 1990 Page 2 RE: Government Code Section 83114(b) The Physical Setting: The proposed specific plan covers a site to be developed in three phases, within a "campus" area bounded by Ramona Street, Channing Avenue, Waverly Street and Homer Avenue, plus a portion of land across the street on Homer Avenue between Bryant Street and Waverly Street. For your information, I have enclosed three pages from the proposed specific plan to show the proposed phased development. (Figures 16, 17 and 18.) I have also enclosed Figure 6 from the proposed specific plan, which shows the lotting pattern of the area as well as the existing land use for all lots in the area. On Figure 6 I have marked the lots in which the two Planning Commissioners have an interest. You will note that Commissioner Cullen's townhouse development is "Catty cornered" from the project site and that a portion of the real property in which Commissioner Chandler has an interest backs up to that part of the proposed project which is across the street from the proposed campus. Legal Analysis:
embedding.summary: This is an incoming request from the City Attorney of Palo Alto to the FPPC seeking advice on whether two Planning Commission members must recuse themselves due to conflicts of interest related to their real property ownership within 300 feet of a proposed PAMF campus development. The request includes background letters from the City Attorney to each commissioner advising them of likely conflicts under Government Code Section 87100 and FPPC regulations.
